title: Ranunculus acutiusculus
taxonomy: Ranunculus acutiusculus is classified within the family Ranunculaceae and the order Ranunculales. It belongs to the kingdom Plantae and the clade Angiosperms. This species is further categorized within the class Magnoliopsida and the division Magnoliophyta. Ranunculus acutiusculus is native to North America and can be found in various parts of the continent. Its classification is based on its morphological characteristics and genetic relationships with other species in the Ranunculaceae family.

side_effects: Ranunculus acutiusculus, also known as the acute water buttercup, contains toxic compounds called ranunculin and acutifolin, which can cause severe symptoms if ingested. If ingested in large amounts, the plant can cause nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, and may also lead to more serious complications like renal failure. The skin of the plant contains urushiol, an oil that can cause an allergic reaction, resulting in skin irritation and blistering. Ingestion of the plant can also cause cardiac arrhythmias, seizures, and even death in extreme cases. It is essential to handle and dispose of Ranunculus acutiusculus properly to avoid any potential health risks.
intro: Ranunculus acutiusculus, also known as sharp-leaved buttercup, is a species of flowering plant in the family Ranunculaceae. Traditionally used in folk medicine, Ranunculus acutiusculus is valued for its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can help alleviate conditions such as arthritis and improve overall immune function. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Ranunculus acutiusculus is commonly prepared into teas, infusions, and tinctures, as well as being used in topical creams and ointments to treat skin conditions and wounds.
